subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Atomic structure determines chemical identity — the arrangement of protons, neutrons, and electrons governs mass, charge, reactivity, and periodic behavior.

core_concepts.title: The Mole and Molar Mass
core_concepts.detail: One mole contains $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ entities (Avogadro's number). Molar mass (g/mol) equals the atomic or molecular mass in grams, allowing conversion between grams and moles using $n = m/M$.

core_concepts.title: Isotopes and Mass Spectrometry
core_concepts.detail: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons (same $Z$, different $A$). Average atomic mass is the weighted average of isotopic masses by natural abundance. Mass spectrometry identifies isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio.

core_concepts.title: Electron Configuration
core_concepts.detail: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy following the Aufbau principle, Hund's rule (maximize parallel spins in degenerate orbitals), and the Pauli exclusion principle (no two electrons share all four quantum numbers). Configuration determines valence electrons and periodic behavior.

core_concepts.title: Photoelectron Spectroscopy (PES)
core_concepts.detail: PES measures ionization energies of electrons in each subshell. Higher binding energy means the electron is closer to the nucleus. The relative peak heights in a PES spectrum reflect the relative number of electrons in each subshell.

core_concepts.title: Empirical and Molecular Formulas
core_concepts.detail: The empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms. The molecular formula is a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula. Percent composition data allows calculation of empirical formulas: divide mass percent by atomic mass, then normalize to smallest whole numbers.

core_concepts.title: Periodic Trends
core_concepts.detail: Across a period (left to right), effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) increases, pulling electrons closer: atomic radius decreases, ionization energy increases, and electronegativity increases. Down a group, additional electron shells increase atomic radius and decrease ionization energy and electronegativity.

core_concepts.title: Valence Electrons and Ion Formation
core_concepts.detail: Valence electrons (outermost shell) determine chemical behavior. Metals tend to lose electrons to form cations; nonmetals tend to gain electrons to form anions. The number of electrons lost or gained relates directly to position in the periodic table.

formulas.name: Moles from mass
formulas.description: Converts grams of a substance to moles by dividing by molar mass.
formulas.example: How many moles in 18.0 g of water ($M = 18.0$ g/mol)? $n = 18.0/18.0 = 1.00$ mol.

formulas.name: Average atomic mass
formulas.description: Weighted average of isotope masses, where $f_i$ is the fractional abundance and $m_i$ is the mass of isotope $i$.

formulas.name: Percent composition
formulas.description: Mass fraction of a given element in a compound, where $n$ is the number of atoms of that element per formula unit.

exam_tip: When given PES data, the leftmost (highest binding energy) peak corresponds to the innermost electrons (e.g., 1s). The number of peaks tells you how many subshells are occupied. CB questions often ask you to match a PES spectrum to an element — count peaks, compare relative heights to electron subshell populations.
